COMPUTER SCIENCE - BS
Texas A&M University
The computer science curriculum is designed to prepare students to enter the rapidly expanding field of computing. The four-year undergraduate curriculum provides sound preparation in computing, science, mathematics, English and statistics. PROGRAM EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES
1. Graduates use computer science principles to identify and solve emerging technological and societal problems ethically. 2. Graduates who enter the workforce become effective leaders and innovators. 3. Graduates who pursue advanced degrees succeed in prestigious graduate programs. 4. Graduates engage in life-long learning to adapt to new technologies.

Graduates who pursue advanced degrees succeed in prestigious graduate programs. 4. Graduates engage in life-long learning to adapt to new technologies. DEGREE PLAN
FIRST YEAR - FALL SEMESTER (16 credit hours) CHEM 107 - General Chemistry for Engineering Students - 3 hours CHEM 117 - General Chemistry for Engineering Students Laboratory - 1 hour ENGL 103 or ENGL 104 - Introduction to Rhetoric and Composition or Composition and Rhetoric - 3 hours ENGR 102 - Engineering Lab I - Computation - 2 hours MATH 151 - Engineering Mathematics I - 4 hours University Core Curriculum elective - 3 hours
FIRST YEAR - SPRING SEMESTER (15-16 credit hours) ENGR 216 / PHYS 216 - Experimental Physics and Engineering Lab II - Mechanics - 2 hours MATH 152 - Engineering Mathematics II - 4 hours PHYS 206 - Newtonian Mechanics for Engineering and Science - 3 hours University Core Curriculum elective - 3 h

ours CHEM 120 - Fundamentals of Chemistry II or University Core Curriculum elective - 3-4 hours

CSCE 181 - Introduction to Computing - 1 hour
CSCE 120 - Program Design and Concepts - 3 hours
CSCE 222 / ECEN 222 - Discrete Structures for Computing - 3 hours
MATH 304 - Linear Algebra - 3 hours

CSCE 313 - Introduction to Computer Systems - 4 hours
CSCE 331 - Foundations of Software Engineering - 4 hours.

- A grade of C or better is required in all CSCE courses. - Of the 18 computer science elective hours: 3 must be systems electives, 3 must be software and languages electives, 3 must be information and applications electives, 9 must be upper-level CS electives.

- Of the 18 computer science elective hours: 3 must be systems electives, 3 must be software and languages electives, 3 must be information and applications electives, 9 must be upper-level CS electives. - Of the 21 University Core Curriculum hours: 3 from creative arts, 3 from social and behavioral sciences, 3 from language/philosophy/culture, 6 from American history, 6 from government/political science. - All students must complete a high-impact experience to graduate. - This program is approved to be offered at Texas A&M University at Galveston campus.

Visit College Station
Events in College Station bring the community and visitors together all year long. From live music and local markets to sporting events, festivals and family-friendly experiences, there is always something happening in the Heart of Aggieland. Whether you are planning a weekend visit or looking to experience the city like a local, College Station’s event calendar offers memorable moments that showcase its energy, traditions and unmistakable sense of place. Depending on the time of year, take advantage of vibrant happenings in entertainment, sports, food, the arts and more.

Parking Frequently Asked Questions
Why don't parking violators receive a warning before they are issued a parking ticket? When looking at the big picture and considering the number of customers who access the campus per year (i.e., 58,920 students, 12,000 faculty and staff, countless visitors), chaos would ensue if they took advantage of parking illegally - even just once. "Warnings" are posted throughout the campus on the signs that read "Permit Required", "24 Hour Reserved", "Fire Lane", etc. In addition, we have found that issuing warnings does not stop actions of violators. We promise to our permit holders that they will be able to find a space in their assigned lot.

In addition, we have found that issuing warnings does not stop actions of violators. We promise to our permit holders that they will be able to find a space in their assigned lot. We want to reward the customers who behave as good stewards and do not negatively impact their fellow Aggies by ensuring that they may park. What can I do if I can’t afford to purchase a parking permit?

We want to reward the customers who behave as good stewards and do not negatively impact their fellow Aggies by ensuring that they may park. What can I do if I can’t afford to purchase a parking permit? There are several options for customers to lower or avoid parking costs: Ride the bus (free) Purchase parking in surface lots instead of garages Park and ride (free) – park at First Baptist Church in College Station and ride the bus for free Use a combination of options and purchase parking only when you need it - by the hour, day or week instead of an annual permit Customers can carpool with one permit, sharing the permit cost.

There are several options for customers to lower or avoid parking costs: Ride the bus (free) Purchase parking in surface lots instead of garages Park and ride (free) – park at First Baptist Church in College Station and ride the bus for free Use a combination of options and purchase parking only when you need it - by the hour, day or week instead of an annual permit Customers can carpool with one permit, sharing the permit cost. With virtual permits, just add the license plate for the vehicle being driven today to the permit and park.

There are several options for customers to lower or avoid parking costs: Ride the bus (free) Purchase parking in surface lots instead of garages Park and ride (free) – park at First Baptist Church in College Station and ride the bus for free Use a combination of options and purchase parking only when you need it - by the hour, day or week instead of an annual permit Customers can carpool with one permit, sharing the permit cost. With virtual permits, just add the license plate for the vehicle being driven today to the permit and park. We do provide several other services to help limit a student’s need to bring a car to town/campus: Weekend shuttles home and back – RedCoach Rideshare, Rent cars by the hour, day or weekend – Zipcar – drivers as low as age 18; gas and insurance are included, Bike share for getting around campus and to nearby necessities – Veo
I read that one of the main reasons parking permits have increased in costs is due to the fact that the parking garages cost so much to build.

We do provide several other services to help limit a student’s need to bring a car to town/campus: Weekend shuttles home and back – RedCoach Rideshare, Rent cars by the hour, day or weekend – Zipcar – drivers as low as age 18; gas and insurance are included, Bike share for getting around campus and to nearby necessities – Veo
I read that one of the main reasons parking permits have increased in costs is due to the fact that the parking garages cost so much to build. If we are paying extra for parking because of this, why do we have to pay by the hour to park in the garages, even at night?

If we are paying extra for parking because of this, why do we have to pay by the hour to park in the garages, even at night? It takes several sources of revenue (annual permits, timed visitor, special event fees and fines) to support the system. Parking auxiliaries at a Texas public university cannot be funded by the state. All costs must be paid for through fees charged to users. If someone utilizes a parking space, they should be paying or paid for. If not, then enforcement methods are employed to assure that the permit holder is not subsidizing this use. It is true that the price of parking permits will rise over time like any other product.

If not, then enforcement methods are employed to assure that the permit holder is not subsidizing this use. It is true that the price of parking permits will rise over time like any other product. Debt service, custodial, maintenance, personnel and equipment costs are significant. Gated access controlled locations are expensive to maintain and operate. Permit holders also hold night and weekend parking privileges which allow for parking outside of their designated lot. Night+ permits have the same access as the night permit as well as nighttime access to ONE of the following garages: University Center, Gene Stallings Blvd, Central Campus, or Polo Rd. See Night & Weekend Parking.

Night+ permits have the same access as the night permit as well as nighttime access to ONE of the following garages: University Center, Gene Stallings Blvd, Central Campus, or Polo Rd. See Night & Weekend Parking. Why do summer school students have to pay for parking when there are fewer students on campus and therefore more empty spaces? Parking is a system. The annual fee for the year is prorated. Due to fewer demands on the system, there are more parking options available during the summer. However, the cost of providing the service does not diminish in the same way student population fluctuates. I heard that the Parking Services Officers are driving vehicles and scooters.

However, the cost of providing the service does not diminish in the same way student population fluctuates. I heard that the Parking Services Officers are driving vehicles and scooters. Why don't they walk or use bicycles to save gas? Parking enforcement is a service that is necessary for the University. As with all departments, parking officers are provided with the equipment necessary to perform the tasks asked of them. Most officers do conduct their patrol on foot. Some ride bikes. Supervisors who are required to oversee larger sections of campus or officers required to deliver or make use of equipment use vehicles that meet these demands.

Supervisors who are required to oversee larger sections of campus or officers required to deliver or make use of equipment use vehicles that meet these demands. With the Texas A&M University campus size, off main campus duties, weather and demands for prompt service, walking and riding bikes simply doesn't always get the job done. How do I park when attending an event (football, basketball, concerts, etc.)? Log in to My Parking and scroll to the bottom of the page. You may print this barcode or show your phone to the lot attendee. The license plate shown with the barcode must match the vehicle entering the lot. For event parking locations, please visit our athletics events and annual events pages.
